transcript.text: Thank you.
Hi, Cindy.
Sorry I'm late.
Hi, no worries.
I was about to email you.
How are you?
Doing great.
Nice to meet you.
Yeah, you as well.
So you're in Argentina?
Yeah.
Yeah, I'm here in Buenos Aires.
Awesome.
One of my favorite.
Have you been here?

Yeah, my husband and I were there for a month, two years ago, and we did like a couple weeks in Buenos Aires, and then we went to Berloche, and then down to Chalten, and like lots of hiking down there.
Ah, it's really beautiful.
I loved it, and it was just like so warm.
were there in November, so it wasn't too hot, but yeah, but it was still like super nice and long nights.
November is a perfect season, because it's like the in-between, and the city looks so beautiful.
Yeah, the flowers, I forget what the flowers are.
It's all blossoming, yeah.

It's very similar to, like I live in Vancouver, British Columbia, so I felt like the country was kind of similar, at least in the south, all the mountains, and like the hiking and the lakes and stuff, super nice.
Yeah, but it's crazy, because I feel like here you get four countries every year.
Like right now, it's a completely different country.
It's getting colder, it's like...
Like full autumn, it rains, so.
Yeah, and then you have like desert up northwest as well.
I would like to go to Fathom at some point as well and then go there.
Yeah, I want to go.
I haven't, because I wasn't born in Argentina, I've been here for like eight years.
So I don't know everything yet, but I really want to travel more in the next few years.
Yeah, where did you live before?
Well, I'm from Venezuela, but I used to live, yeah, I used to live here in Argentina in the south of Argentina.
In a city called Neuquén, it's really similar to Bariloche.
If you've been to the south of Argentina, all the cities are really similar.
Yeah.
But I used to live down there and it was really beautiful too.
Awesome, cool.
Well, I'm excited to meet you.
I watched your video and I've looked at your resume and everything.
So let me give you more first background on me and the company and then I'd like to ask you.
More about your background as well.
But so I've been at GrowthX for like only two and a half months, but I'm one of the earlier people just because we've been growing a lot since then.
And so the company is really today.
The company looks more like a content marketing agency.
So today, like our customers hire us to produce a lot of content for them and help them with SEO, like programmatic SEO as well.
And then our team is using like different AI workflows we've built to produce that content so that it's like we can produce like a high volume using the AI.
And then, of course, we still have a lot of people involved to make that are like checking it, prompting it better, improving like the input so that the output of the AI is better.
And like that whole process is constantly getting better.
So and then like so the customer is not like using a product today with us.
It's really just interfacing with our team and then our team does the work.
So that's what it's today.
But the idea is that we're going to start trying to build more of a platform and a product where they could potentially log in and maybe run some of the stuff themselves.
So the company is early.
It's only like eight months old.
So we have plenty of options ahead of us.
So that's sort of what the business is today.
And then the team, like we obviously have like operations, people, and also engineers building kind of the product side.
And then we have the majority of the team today is like all of the agency side, which is all of the people working for the customers.
So we're 50 people today.
Okay.
Yeah, when I joined, we were like 18.
And that was like two and a half months ago.
yeah.
And then the team is spread all over the world.
Like the biggest, the most people we have in a country is going to be the US for sure.
And then maybe Canada.
Then after that, it's kind of like we have three or four people in many different places.
We have one person, at least one in Buenos Aires already.
We started like two weeks ago.
So anyway, we're hiring everywhere, which is fun and makes it so that the team is super diverse and interesting.
So anyway, there's just obviously a lot going on.
Like not only is the team growing like 20 to 50 people in a few months, but the customers are growing and the revenue is growing as well.
So like expectations are growing from customers.
So it's just changing fast and like doing very well, but like it's a lot and everyone is relatively new.
And so there's just like, there's just, we just don't have enough like help on those, on the ops team more than anything.
Like helping the agency side is kind of doing its own thing and the engineers, but like operationally, like helping with all of the different leadership members.
And like teams to just like build leverage for them.
So that's why I just started last week.

Just I reached I've been working with near agency just looking for like, super awesome, EA VA, whatever you want to call it slash like, just people who are willing to do a bunch of random stuff because like, every day is different here.
And so while there will be more like, you know, there's gonna be ongoing stuff that's similar with like, you know, email, scheduling, all of that.
There's also going to be a ton of like, variety and projects that come up.
So definitely looking for people who are either used to that kind of environment or excited by that environment, which is like, for sure, you won't be bored here.
It's more about like dealing with overwhelming amount of things going on.
So anyway, I'll pause there.
And if you have any initial questions, let me know.
Otherwise, we can dive into your background as well.
don't, let me
Sure.
I wanted to ask, top of my head, you said that you want people to help the high charges and different teams.
Do you know how many high charges do you have in the business or in how many teams do you divide the business?
I just want to have an idea of how the structure of the business works.
Yeah.
So we have, if we have 50 people today, I would say probably like 30 of those 50 are in different roles, but they're all like customer facing.
Okay.
And there's a few tiers there of like, like levels.
There's a few levels there from director to like a manager to like associate level, whatever you want to call it.
So that's probably 30 of the 50 people is like customer facing.
And then there's, let's say my team is ops.
So we do, uh, HR, finance, legal, IT, uh, recruiting.
Yeah.
So it's like, there's probably six or.
Seven of us.
then we've got also the engineers.
There's like between five and 10 engineers today.
And then so on the leadership team, which is where I'm ideally I'd like to have like a partner for each person on the leadership team.
So there's six of us.
So there's our chief content officer, and he is leading the entire agency board.
There's our head of sales.
There's our head of marketing, basically.
Growth is what his title is.
And then there's our CEO, our CTO, leading tech, and then me.
So I'm actually trying, like I'm working with Nir.
I'm trying to find like a partner for each person on the team.
And we already have one for the CTO.
She already works with us.
So yeah, so like definitely I need to find, and everybody has like a little bit of a niche background and niche needs and stuff.
But yeah, generally, it's just like Yeah.
You know, all around generalist style people who are excited to take on.
Yeah, hope that helps.
Yeah, yeah.
It's an important question because it varies a lot.
Like if you work with a single person versus assisting only you, like four different people.
No, I don't think that's possible.
different type of job.
Yeah, and like, I don't think that scales well.
I don't think that anybody would be set up for success if they had to do that.
But so I would rather have like one-to-one.
I mean, let's see how it works out.
But like, that would be my ideal because then also you can like take on more and more responsibility and you can grow as well in your career.
Like where instead of just getting stuck doing like, that would be a full-time job just doing schedules and emails for four people.
But instead, if you have time outside of that for your main principle, you can do with tons of new things.
So for me, I also see it as an opportunity to like really learn and level up from like someone who's...
Our chief content officer ran TechCrunch, which is a huge publication.
He ran it for 10 years.
That would be a very cool thing to work and learn from him.
Our CEO was CMO at a ton of companies, and you get to learn.
Anywho, let's hear about you.
I'd love to hear about your background and what makes you interested in this role and what path you're on or thinking about.
Well, first of all, I want to mention that I'm a psychology student.
Everything regarding HR and operations is really interesting for me.
In all the positions I've had in the past, I've leaned towards operations in general.
Everything regarding customer management, CRMs, calendar management, email management.
I've also handled creating content for different platforms like LinkedIn.
I used to have a blog.
Really good, according to me.
So yeah, well, my first, I've been an assistant for the last six years, but I'd say that my first really interesting job was at U, the skincare brand.
I don't know if you've heard from them.
Anyways, it's a brand from the States.
I was their first employee, actually, and the team grew so much in my time there.
I had a really good relationship.
I used to assist the three founders from a more operational, administrative perspective.
And then we have another assistant that's one of my best friends to this day, and she was more in charge of the marketing side of the business.
So it was a really good team type of situation.
We had communication every single day.
And it was a really fun experience because that's when I learned to have the type of position that changes every single day.
Instead of having repetitive work, which is...
What I might have had in the past.
But in this position, it was like everything was new, everything was growing.
They're in retail selling, so it's really crazy to be able to have everything in stock at the same time.
I was in charge of ordering the stock, so I would need to keep track of a lot of things.
Since it was the first year of the business, we needed to implement a lot of systems.
They didn't have any standardized organization or procedures for anything, I'd say, because they were just starting.
So I helped them.
Yeah, I helped them implement that.
And I feel like that that experience has helped me in all of my other jobs or with all of my other customers, because it was really like from scratch.
And now it's a really huge skincare brand.
They're already in Sephora in different countries.
Yeah, so it was it was.
It's a really good, like, perspective from, for a business that grew really fast.
And I had, I was mainly the assistant to a CEO.
So, and she's an impressive woman.
So she taught me, like, every single thing.
God bless her.
She was so patient with me.
So, yeah, I would say that in a quick sum up, I don't want to take too much time because I know you must have questions.
But in a quick sum up, I would say that's what I would highlight of my experience.
What have you been doing?
Because that was two years ago.
So what have you been doing since?
Is it, are you with one client now?
Or how does the last two years look?
I've been freelancing.
So, yeah, right now I'm with a single customer.
At some point I had two.
It was too hectic for me.
So I left one.
Right now I'm with only one.
But it's more of a freelancing type of situation.
It's not like a full-time job.
And that's what I am looking, actually.
What kind of a company is that?
It's an...
Architectural business.
So it's also like a really small business, but it's a different type because it's not a, you're not selling a tangible product.
So you have to come up with a ton of different strategies to sell and to market.
So yeah.
Yeah.
Okay.
Awesome.

And it sounds like you liked the skincare company in that it was like always changing and like, I'm sure it's stressful and chaotic at times, but like, seems like it provided lots of opportunities to learn new things.
Yeah.
I'll, I'm the type of person that gets bored if I do the same thing every single day.
And I love finding like solutions to problem.
Like, I don't at the top of my head, when we were in, when I was working in Dew, um, we would have this issue with the, because you have to order, they created the skincare products.
So, you know, some of you.
You have to order all of the materials, make sure they get there on time before you actually make the product.
And if this has like a day of delay, then you won't have anything to sell.
So in my time there, we implemented a system that would order all of the raw materials automatically for us in the time.
And I was in charge of setting that up.
So that was extremely interesting for me.
Like see how like the operational side of the business works from scratch.
It's really interesting.
Like I really get bored if I do the same thing every single day.
So for me, this is fascinating.
I know that for some people, this is kind of boring, like the operational, but for me, it's really the most interesting.
assembly line stuff.
That's like true deep, like product operations too, which I've never done anything like that.
Because I've always worked in tech companies where there's no like physical product being served.
So that's a whole different type of operations.
It's totally, yeah, it's really different.
And
Right now, for this company that I'm at, I also do all the operations, but it's a whole different thing.
Sorry, my annoying dog.
Can you hear him?
Sorry.
No, I couldn't hear.
Okay, I'm glad.
What are you most interested in, like five years from now?
What kind of roles do you think you would like to do?
I mean, definitely something related to HR and with working with teams and operational.
That would be like my, so your job, basically.
That sounds really interesting to me.
Or something along those lines that I help optimize teams, maybe.
So I would say like the inner side of HR sounds really interesting to me.
Yeah, like that's what I like is there's, so our founders are obviously like more product, like very product visionary and they're figuring out how to make.

What we build resonate with the outside world and the customers, whereas my team is like, we need to make sure everyone, all 50 people or 100 people in the company are actually enabled to be able to do all of that with different systems and processes and all of that, too.
Yeah.
And what companies don't realize is that if you focus, because some startups, and it's understandable, they don't want to dedicate a budget to the inner side of the team.
They think everything works and that everything's okay.
But once you start moving a few pieces and everything starts working better, it makes a huge difference on the team.
Yeah, I agree.
That's really interesting to me.
What's, like, one of the most challenging projects you've had?
And challenging in that it was, like, super undefined and you didn't, like, someone asked you to do something kind of vague and you had to figure out what it meant and how to do it.
Well, it happens to me right now with this client, because she doesn't have, like, a really, a structure.
Architecture in place.
Architecture is really complex.
It's like if you get two projects, two big projects per year, that's a lot.
So you work a lot before you even see actual sales.
So you never know if anything's working.

So the first thing that I did to try to address that, I don't know if this answers your question, but the first thing that I did when I came in the team was to make sure that all the procedures were completely standardized.
We have folders in place that all the team can go to of what they should be doing, when they should be doing, all of their KPIs, and everyone has access to that.
So once we were, they didn't even have had like KPIs before, because she thought she couldn't measure things.
But once I came in the team, I told her, there's always things you can measure.
It's probably not going to be sales, because you only have two per year, but there's other types of stuff.
So once we had that in place, we were actually, we were actually able to...
To predict what was going to happen through the year and be proactive, which is ultimately...
some examples of that?
Like, what would be some example KPIs that her business would track?
I don't know.
For example, at the top of my mind, for marketing, we knew that if she has, like, 20 meetings, out of those 20 meetings, who would lead to actual proposals?
Okay, I see.
And then make sure she has enough meetings.
Make absolutely sure that at least in the first semester, she has 100, I don't know, or 50, whatever.
Because she didn't even know that that's how she actually got sales.
And before us, she, like, in the first year, we would actually do, had the girls, the marketing girls do a lot of efforts.
And she thought that it wasn't working because we weren't tracking it.
Yeah.
So it's really important to have, like, everything in place and make sure that...
We always say that we have everything...
The whole procedure that we have, it has to have a point.
Like, if we can't explain it, we shouldn't have it.
Yeah, I agree.
For sure.
That's the most important thing.
I don't know if this answers your question at all.
No, no, I agree with you.
No, I was more just using it as the means to just see, like, how you think about things.
But I agree, like, process for the sake of processes really slows you down.

And then the other thing I believe is, like, in a company that grows fast like ours, I almost want to question every process every three months just for, because there's just should be no way that it's still relevant if we've, like, added 10.
Yeah, like, so for me, I always think about it with meetings.
I'm like, say we have, like, a recurring meeting and we have the same agenda, and it's the same thing six months later.
Like, we're not doing something right.
It's like, why are we still talking in this manner about the same thing?
It's like, let's, what else should we be talking To, like, take us to the next level.
So, I agree.
Um, what, um, what's some feedback you've received, both positive and also something constructive, like something you were told to work on?
Something I got told to work on a lot by my first boss at Duke.
She would tell me I forgot things a lot.
So I started writing every single thing down.
Yeah.
She always told me, like, I know you forgot about this, but you had to do this, like, today.
And I was like, no.
And it was my first experience.
So I didn't have the habit of, like, writing every single thing I needed to do down and be specific.
Or I would forget the details of what I had to do.
So now I'm like, I have, like, the best notes.
Like, I write every single thing down that doesn't happen to me ever.
And positive feedback.
Well, Samantha, which is my current client, she always says I have creative solutions.
Yeah.
I have really creative to solving problems.
really firm in process.
So I guess, yeah, that's a good one.

Like thinking outside the box, because especially if you want to be like a, like innovative company and as a whole, then you need to make sure like internally, everybody is thinking about like, how can I do this process either faster or better?
Like everything I do is like, okay, I'm, I've been doing this.
I'm doing the same thing over and over 10 times.
Like, how can I make it?
So that's not the case.
Or like, I spend an hour on this thing every week.
Like, how can I make it?
So it's 30 minutes, you know what I mean?
So like that kind of mindset.
Yeah, I think one thing that I'm trying to like avoid in anybody I hire is like, people who assume that they only have the resources that exist today to get their job done.
And it's like, if you if you can think of something that you need to do it, like, let's talk about it.
If you need to hire your own freelancer.
For some tasks that's super going to be take you a week, you know, like, or if you need a tool, or there's like, you need somebody else to help you with something.
It's like, don't be constrained by yourself and your own time.
You know, if it's like, you're getting overwhelmed by some big project, like, okay, how can we, what are some other things I can tap into to leverage also for you?
So yeah, there's like, I, you know, I don't want people to get stuck like, oh, I don't know how I can do this.
Yeah, I feel like if you're in the startup world, you'll be used to that because that's usually how business operates.
They're open to new ideas and stuff.
But I feel that people that come from really large corporations might not be used to that because it's like a fixed position that you can only do a certain amount of things.
Yeah, large or even just like older industries.
Like my first job was in like an investment management firm, and it was only 50 people.
But like, every time I suggested to change.
And something they'd be like, well, I don't know, we kind of already do it this way.
And it's like, okay, but you're not listening.
I am suggesting a better way to do it.
So I found that also frustrating.
And it's just like, attracts people to the startup world as well.
I want to ask you, so like, it sounds like you're most interested in like off stuff and like my org.
Oh, I did want to ask you, because I also think you would be great with the CEO as well.
Like I just wanted to gauge your interest there and like his world to give you a glimpse into his world.
And maybe look him up on LinkedIn.
His name is, I can send it to you in the chat.
But like, he's super awesome, legit.
Like, let me paste his profile in.
Super nice.
He's actually Brazilian.
So he grew up in Brazil, but then moved to the US.
So he's like a CMO at all of these, like chief marketing officer at like all these super legit companies.
like, going

What his world is today, and I'll just leave this with you and then, like, think about it and just, like, get back to me, but also happy to, like, I really like chatting with you, so regardless, I think we should, like, do another chat, but his world is, like, super busy, obviously, but he does, he spends a lot of time, one, with investors.
So, like, we get hit up by so many venture capitalists, and so he's spending time investing in those relationships, so I would say that's a portion of his week.

The second is he basically does most of our sales today, so he's always meeting with prospective customers, because the way we get business, he literally, he has 15,000 followers on LinkedIn, so he'll just post, and we'll get, like, 10 leads that are, like, I want to work with you in your new company.
So, he's in a lot of sales.
Sorry, I was going to ask, who handles his LinkedIn, like, with the same system you have?
No, like, he does, which is...
It's not good because he gets so many replies.
He has like hundreds of LinkedIn messages.
And what I'm worried about, what we're both worried about, is that like he'll post something like even if he posts, hey, we're hiring engineers, check out our job postings.
Then he'll get hundreds of messages.
And it's like 90% of those I'm sure we can just mark as red delete.
But then there's probably 10% that are like amazing candidates and amazing or investors or prospective customers.
And like so we need someone to help him who can understand, can take like the extra initiative to like look at the person or look at the company and be like, you know what?
I think this is worth your time kind of thing versus like today.
think he just ends up having to like go through all of them.
And if someone could like save him time by going through some of that, that would be great.
So that's kind of one thing also is the LinkedIn and then like writing posts as well.
Like he wants to post at least.
One to two times a week because he gets like a whole million responses and views and everything.
So like helping draft posts and content and all that.
And then just like other random things, like he's just always kind of has all these ideas and is like looking for someone to help look into them for him, you know, like, oh, what if we did?
What if I did a podcast?
Okay, what would that look like?
And then like, you know, so for him, I'm looking, it's going to be more like a mini chief of staff role where it's like, okay, I want to do a podcast.
Like I would have to go, okay, research, like, are there similar podcasts out there today?
How would that work?
What does it entail?
What are the costs?
How much time would that take out of his life?
Do we really want to do that now?
Probably have to talk to other people on the executive team.
Like, does this make sense now?
How is this going to bring, you know, like, not that you have to make the decision, but just like map out a little bit of like what.
What this project could look like, and then he can decide if it's worth his time.
like general research and reporting for different projects.
Yeah, and then my org, of course, is going to be way more like what I mentioned.
I've got legal, which is just outside counsel, but I'm working with them.
Recruiters, two recruiters.
Currently, I'm doing most of the people ops with the recruiters because it's mostly hiring right now.

So that's what our world looks like.
It's much more like internal.
We're doing a lot of like, how can we make everybody's life easier in Notion and tools we use so the whole company is like doing the same stuff.
So that's kind of my world.
But I do, I'm pretty sure I have a meeting right now.
Hold on, me just check my calendar.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

Discuss potential EA/VA role at GrowthX with candidate Cindy Castillejo

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX is a rapidly growing content marketing agency (50 employees, up from 18 in 2.5 months) transitioning to a SaaS platform model](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=160.0)
  - [Seeking EAs/VAs to support leadership team members 1:1, handle varied tasks, and grow with the company](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=487.0)
  - [Cindy has 6 years of assistant experience, including ops/admin for a fast-growing skincare startup](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=794.0)
  - [Strong interest in HR, operations, and team optimization aligns well with GrowthX needs](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=1101.0)

## Topics

### Company Overview

  - [Team structure: 30 customer-facing, 6-7 ops, 5-10 engineers, 6 leadership members](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=559.0)
  - [Global team, revenue growing rapidly](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=443.0)

### Role Requirements

  - [Seeking EAs/VAs to partner 1:1 with leadership team members](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=487.0)
  - [Need for adaptable generalists comfortable with varied, evolving tasks](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=517.0)
  - [Opportunity to grow and take on increased responsibility](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=694.0)
  - [Key areas: email/calendar management, project research, process improvement](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=487.0)

### Cindy's Background

  - [Psychology student with 6 years assistant experience](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=742.0)
  - [Strengths in operations, CRM, content creation](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=761.0)
  - [Key experience: First employee at U skincare brand, assisting 3 founders](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=798.0)
  - [Implemented systems/processes from scratch as company rapidly scaled](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=864.0)
  - [Currently freelancing for architectural firm, seeking full-time role](https://fathom.video/share/hXncRJoRnum5S6eNFXzMFX73qAWM2-gc?tab=summary&timestamp=935.0)

### Career Aspirations
